notes.anchor: His place at Falernum has always struck me as a suitable inn
notes.note: Falernum (ager Falernus) is the famous wine-producing district on the border of Campania and Latium; Macula apparently had a property there that could serve as a way-station (deversorium) for a travelling party, though Cicero doubts it has room enough.

Lepta's estate at Petrinum (location uncertain, possibly in Campania), which Cicero visited and evidently valued as a proper country villa for an extended stay, contrasted with the mere overnight usefulness of Macula's farm.

notes.anchor: some sort of curatorship of the royal games
notes.note: After Caesar's conquest of the East, a curatorship (curatio) of the 'royal games' (ludi regii) was a prize Caesarian appointment — oversight of lavish games funded from confiscated royal treasuries. Cicero counsels Lepta not to seek it via Oppius or Balbus, since Caesar's inner circle was already over-subscribed and no one who brought only goodwill (rather than money or concrete usefulness) would get in.

notes.anchor: the favour (if indeed he learns of you at all)
notes.note: Cicero articulates the asymmetry of favour under a monarchy: when the ruler gives an appointment, the obligation runs entirely toward him, and the recipient is in debt rather than creditor. The observation has a wry edge given Cicero's own situation.
